coredata.dc:description: The present study evaluates the anticorrosive performance of a novel benzodiazepine derivative, PNO2, on mild steel in an aggressive 15 % HCl medium at 298 K. A combination of gravimetric measurements, potentiodynamic polarisation, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to quantify the corrosion inhibition efficiency at different concentrations of PNO2. Surface characterization techniques, including Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), were employed to examine the morphology and chemical interactions on the steel surface after immersion. The results revealed a significant decrease in corrosion rate and current density upon addition of the inhibitor, with maximum inhibition efficiencies reaching 84.8 % and 84.4 % based on gravimetric and electrochemical data, respectively, at 10−3 M. The Nyquist plots demonstrated an increased charge transfer resistance and reduced double-layer capacitance, confirming the formation of a protective film. AFM analysis showed a notable reduction in surface roughness, while SEM/EDX spectra confirmed the adsorption of PNO2. Furthermore, adsorption studies indicated that PNO2 follows the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, with ΔG°ads values supporting a spontaneous chemisorption mechanism. Overall, PNO2 exhibited strong inhibitory action through mixed-type inhibitor, even under highly acidic conditions, making it a promising candidate for corrosion control in industrial environments.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study evaluates the anticorrosive performance of a novel benzodiazepine derivative, PNO2, on mild steel in an aggressive 15 % HCl medium at 298 K. A combination of gravimetric measurements, potentiodynamic polarisation, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to quantify the corrosion inhibition efficiency at different concentrations of PNO2. Surface characterization techniques, including Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), were employed to examine the morphology and chemical interactions on the steel surface after immersion. The results revealed a significant decrease in corrosion rate and current density upon addition of the inhibitor, with maximum inhibition efficiencies reaching 84.8 % and 84.4 % based on gravimetric and electrochemical data, respectively, at 10−3 M. The Nyquist plots demonstrated an increased charge transfer resistance and reduced double-layer capacitance, confirming the formation of a protective film. AFM analysis showed a notable reduction in surface roughness, while SEM/EDX spectra confirmed the adsorption of PNO2. Furthermore, adsorption studies indicated that PNO2 follows the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, with ΔG°ads values supporting a spontaneous chemisorption mechanism. Overall, PNO2 exhibited strong inhibitory action through mixed-type inhibitor, even under highly acidic conditions, making it a promising candidate for corrosion control in industrial environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel corrosion in acidic environments, particularly in hydrochloric acid (HCl) solutions, remains a significant challenge across various industrial sectors, including chemical processing, steel pickling, petroleum refining, and equipment maintenance [1,2]. This corrosion leads to substantial economic losses, structural degradation, and a reduced lifespan of metallic infrastructure [3,4]. To mitigate these adverse effects, the use of organic corrosion inhibitors has emerged as a widely adopted strategy to protect metals in aggressive acidic environments [5]. Organic inhibitors primarily act by adsorbing onto the metal surface, forming a protective film that limits the access of aggressive ions to the metal substrate [6,7]. Among the organic compounds studied, heterocyclic molecules containing heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen, and sulphur exhibit remarkable inhibition efficiency due to their strong adsorption capability and ability to form stable complexes with active sites on the metal surface [8–10]. Several studies have demonstrated that heterocyclic compounds, such as triazoles [11], imidazoles [12], and quinoxaline derivatives [13], exhibit excellent corrosion inhibition properties in acidic solutions. In particular, the use of concentrated HCl solutions such as 15 % HCl is representative of real conditions encountered in industrial pickling and descaling operations, where corrosion inhibitors must perform under highly aggressive environments [14,15].

In this context, benzodiazepines and benzoxazepines have recently garnered increasing attention due to their electron-rich π-systems and ability to establish strong interactions with the metal surface through coordination bonds and π-stacking interactions [16,17]. The work of Verma et al. [16] demonstrated that benzodiazepine derivatives effectively inhibit mild steel corrosion in HCl solutions, with efficiencies often exceeding 90 %. Similarly, Kharbouch et al. [18] studied the performance of benzoxazepines in moderately acidic environments, highlighting their adsorption behaviour, which follows the Langmuir model, and their ability to form stable protective layers. However, most existing studies have focused on moderately acidic environments, primarily 1 M HCl, where these inhibitors have shown optimal performance [19,20]. In more aggressive industrial environments, such as 15 % HCl solutions, the efficiency of organic inhibitors may be significantly reduced due to the accelerated dissolution of the protective film, the deprotonation of inhibitor molecules, and increased competition with hydronium ions [21,22]. Therefore, the chemical stability and adsorption behaviour of these inhibitors in highly acidic conditions require thorough evaluation to ensure their long-term effectiveness in extreme industrial environments. To further emphasize the novelty of the present study, Table 1 provides a comparison of PNO2 with other organic inhibitors reported in the literature and tested under similarly aggressive conditions (15 % HCl). While several compounds show high inhibition efficiency in this medium, PNO2 demonstrates a competitive performance and is distinguished by its structural framework and surface interactions. This comparison reinforces the relevance of PNO2 as a corrosion inhibitor suitable for highly acidic industrial environments.

The present study aims to investigate the corrosion inhibition performance of a newly synthesized benzodiazepine derivative under highly aggressive acidic conditions. For this purpose, the behaviour of mild steel in 15 % HCl solution, both in the absence and presence of the inhibitor, was systematically examined using a combination of weight loss measurements, potentiodynamic polarisation, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. To gain deeper insights into the inhibitor–metal interactions and surface modifications, advanced surface analysis techniques, including Scanning electron microscopy (SEM/EDX) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), were employed. This comprehensive approach provides a robust evaluation of the inhibitor's efficiency, adsorption behaviour, and protective film formation on the steel surface, offering valuable information for its potential application in highly corrosive industrial environments.

The corrosive medium was prepared by diluting analytical-grade hydrochloric acid (37 % purity) with distilled water to obtain a final concentration of 15 % HCl. The inhibitor under investigation, referred to as PNO2 , was synthesized following the procedure outlined in our previous study [26]. The abbreviation, nomenclature, and molecular structure of PNO2 are presented in Table 2 .

The mild steel employed in this investigation had the chemical composition detailed in Table 3 . To ensure consistent surface conditions and reproducibility of results, the working electrodes were carefully polished using emery paper with progressively finer grit sizes (ranging from 40 to 2000). Following this, the electrodes were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water, degreased with acetone, and dried at room temperature before each experiment [27].

Gravimetric analysis were conducted to assess the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency of PNO2 at different concentrations in the 15 % HCl solution. This technique, widely used in corrosion studies, has been validated as a reliable method for evaluating inhibitor performance in acidic environments [28,29]. The corrosion rate (CR) was determined using the following formula:

where CR0 and CRi represent the corrosion rates (mg/cm2. h) before and after the addition of the inhibitor, respectively.

The electrochemical procedures and scientific software applied in this study follow the same methodology established in our previous work, ensuring consistency and methodological reliability across both investigations [30]. The Potentiodynamic polarisation measurements were performed using a PGZ100 potentiostat/galvanostat controlled by VoltaMaster 4 software. This technique is commonly used in corrosion studies to evaluate the anodic and cathodic reactions at the metal-electrolyte interface [31]. The cathodic polarisation curves were obtained by shifting the potential negatively from the corrosion potential (Ecorr), whereas the anodic polarisation curves were recorded by scanning the potential in the opposite direction. A scan rate of 1 mV/s was applied, covering a potential range of −900 mV to −100 mV relative to the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) [32]. The inhibition efficiency (ηPP) was determined from the corrosion current densities using the following equation:
(3)

where I0 and I represent the corrosion current density before and after the addition of the inhibitor.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out at open circuit potential across a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz, using a sinusoidal AC signal with an amplitude of 10 mV. This method is frequently applied to evaluate charge transfer resistance and the formation of protective films on metal surfaces. Prior to the measurements, the mild steel samples were immersed in the corrosive medium for 30 min to ensure a stabilised system [33,34]. The inhibition efficiency (ηEIS) was calculated based on the charge transfer resistance (Rct) using the following equation:

Where R0 ct and Rct are the charge transfer resistances before and after the addition of the inhibitor, respectively.

Surface analyses were carried out using SEM/EDX and AFM techniques after 6 h of immersion of mild steel in 15 % HCl solution at 298 K, with and without 10−3 M of PNO2 . uses a Quanta FEG 450 at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV, to do SEM/EDX analysis. AFM measurements were performed using a NaioAFM (Nanosurf) in dynamic mode to evaluate surface roughness and morphological changes.

The corrosion rate of mild steel in a 15 % HCl solution was measured after 6 h of immersion at 298 K, with and without varying concentrations of PNO2 . Each data point represents the mean value of three independent weight loss measurements, which exhibited excellent reproducibility as confirmed by the low variation between trials. The results, summarised in Table 4 , clearly show a progressive decrease in corrosion rate with increasing concentrations of PNO2 .

As shown in Table 4, the corrosion rate decreases significantly with increasing concentrations of PNO2 . At the lowest concentration of 10−6 M, the corrosion rate is reduced from 9.4125 mg/cm2. h (mean value of three trials in uninhibited 15 % HCl) to 1.9109 mg/cm2. h (mean value of three trials), indicating an inhibition efficiency of 79.7 %. With increasing concentrations of PNO2, the corrosion rate continues to decline, reaching its lowest value of 1.4312 mg/cm2. h (mean value of three trials) at 10−3 M, corresponding to an inhibition efficiency of 84.8 %. This reduction in the corrosion rate can be attributed to the progressive adsorption of PNO2 molecules on the mild steel surface, forming a dense and protective layer that limits the dissolution of the metal in the acidic medium [35]. The strong interaction between the inhibitor molecules and the metal surface is facilitated by the presence of nitrogen and oxygen atoms in the PNO2 structure, which have a high tendency to coordinate with the active sites of the metal. This adsorption mechanism is consistent with the formation of a protective film that blocks the access of aggressive chloride ions to the steel surface, thereby reducing the corrosion rate [36,37].

The potentiodynamic polarisation curves presented for PNO2 in 15 % HCl solution at 298 K (Fig. 1 ) alongside the corresponding electrochemical parameters summarised in Table 5 showcase the influence of PNO2 concentration on mild steel corrosion. The polarisation curves indicate a decrease in current densities with increasing concentrations of PNO2 , suggesting the role of PNO2 as a mixed-type inhibitor. The displacement of the corrosion potential (Ecorr) is less than 85 mV relative to the blank solution, confirming the classification of PNO2 as a mixed-type inhibitor that affects both anodic and cathodic reactions [38–40].

Table 5 highlights the impact of increasing PNO2 concentrations on the corrosion current density (icorr) and inhibition efficiency (η PP). A significant decrease in icorr is observed with rising PNO2 concentrations, indicating the formation of a protective film on the steel surface. At 10−3 M, the corrosion current density is reduced from 3850 μA cm−2 (in uninhibited 15 % HCl) to 600 μA cm−2, resulting in an inhibition efficiency of 84.4 %. This reduction suggests that PNO2 forms a dense, adherent layer that effectively impedes electrochemical reactions at the steel surface [41,42].

The corrosion potential (Ecorr) shows a slight positive shift with increasing concentrations of PNO2 , ranging from 531 mV/SCE at 10−6 M to 529 mV/SCE at 10−3 M, compared to 487 mV/SCE in uninhibited 15 % HCl. Since this shift remains below the 85 mV threshold, PNO2 is confirmed as a mixed-type inhibitor, influencing both anodic and cathodic reactions without altering the dominant reaction mechanism [43–45]. The cathodic (βc) and anodic (βa) Tafel slopes remain relatively stable across different PNO2 concentrations, with values ranging from 122 to 146 mV/dec, indicating that PNO2 adsorption occurs primarily through a surface-blocking mechanism [46–48].

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results presented in Fig. 2 a illustrate the Nyquist plots obtained for mild steel immersed in 15 % HCl with and without PNO2 at various concentrations. The plots exhibit well-defined semicircles, characteristic of charge transfer-controlled processes. In the absence of PNO2 , the diameter of the semicircle is small, reflecting low charge transfer resistance (Rct) and minimal corrosion protection. Upon introducing PNO2 , the semicircle diameter increases progressively with increasing inhibitor concentration, indicating a significant enhancement in Rct values due to the adsorption of PNO2 molecules on the steel surface. This adsorption reduces the active sites available for corrosion, thereby impeding the electrochemical reactions responsible for metal dissolution [49,50].

The Bode modulus and phase angle plots, shown in Fig. 2b and c, provide additional insight into the system's response across the frequency range. The modulus plot highlights higher impedance values with increasing PNO2 concentrations, confirming the improved barrier properties imparted by the inhibitor [51]. Similarly, the phase angle plot exhibits a shift toward higher angles, indicating enhanced capacitive behaviour. This shift suggests the formation of a protective film on the metal surface, which stabilizes and thickens with increasing PNO2 concentrations [52].

The electrochemical parameters derived from the Nyquist plots, summarised in Table 6 , confirm the strong inhibitory effect of PNO2 on mild steel corrosion in 15 % HCl. In the absence of the inhibitor, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is limited to 5.56 Ω cm2, suggesting minimal protection. However, the addition of PNO2 leads to a significant increase in Rct, reaching a maximum value of 35.6 Ω cm2 at 10−3 M. This increase corresponds to an inhibition efficiency (ηimp) of 84.4 %, highlighting the ability of PNO2 to form a dense and adherent protective film on the mild steel surface [53]. The reduction in double-layer capacitance (Cdl) with increasing PNO2 concentrations further supports the adsorption of inhibitor molecules onto the metal surface. The decrease in Cdl from 142.8 μF cm−2 in the uninhibited solution to 89.4 μF cm−2 at 10−3 M suggests that the protective layer formed by PNO2 reduces the dielectric constant and increases the thickness of the double layer, limiting ion transfer and enhancing corrosion resistance [54].

Further investigations were carried out to explore the adsorption behaviour of PNO2 onto the mild steel surface in 15 % HCl solution. The experimental data were fitted to different adsorption models (Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, and Frumkin). Among these, the best correlation was obtained with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, as expressed in equation (5):
(5)

where Cinh is the inhibitor concentration, θ the surface coverage, and Kads the adsorption equilibrium constant. The corresponding linear plot (Fig. 3 ) displayed excellent linearity with an R2 value of 0.99999, confirming that PNO2 adsorption follows the Langmuir model. The adsorption equilibrium constant (Kads) was calculated to be 8.08 × 105 L mol−1, highlighting the strong affinity of PNO2 molecules towards the mild steel surface [55].

The standard Gibbs free energy of adsorption (ΔGads) was determined using equation (6):
(6)

The calculated value of ΔGads was −43.7 kJ mol−1, indicating that the adsorption of PNO2 involves predominantly chemisorption, characterized by strong interaction and charge transfer between the inhibitor molecules and the metallic surface [56]. The comparative fitting of different adsorption models is summarised in Table 7 , where the Langmuir model provided the best correlation compared to Freundlich, Temkin, and Frumkin models.

The long-term performance of PNO2 was assessed by performing EIS measurements at different immersion times, as depicted in Fig. 4 . The Nyquist plots obtained for mild steel in 15 % HCl over immersion periods ranging from 0.5 h to 12 h show a gradual increase in Rct values in the presence of PNO2 . In contrast, the plots for the uninhibited solution exhibit small semicircles throughout the immersion period, indicating continuous metal dissolution [57].

The electrochemical parameters, listed in Table 8 , demonstrate that in the absence of PNO2 , R
ct remains low and relatively constant. Conversely, in the presence of PNO2 , Rct increases progressively with immersion time, reaching 636.2 Ω cm2 after 12 h, suggesting that the protective film formed by PNO2 becomes more compact and stable over time [58]. This increase in Rct with immersion time reflects the gradual adsorption of PNO2 molecules and the subsequent formation of a denser and more protective layer that limits ion diffusion and corrosion processes.

The observed decrease in double-layer capacitance (Cdl) over time further corroborates the enhanced stability of the inhibitor film. As the immersion period increases, Cdl decreases from 89.4 μF cm−2 after 0.5 h to 35.9 μF cm−2 after 12 h, indicating a continuous reduction in the active surface area available for corrosion. This reduction confirms the sustained adsorption of PNO2 molecules, leading to the formation of a thicker and more protective barrier [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature effect and activation parameters of the corrosion process
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on the corrosion behaviour of mild steel in 15 % HCl with and without PNO2 was assessed using potentiodynamic polarisation measurements conducted at temperatures ranging from 298 K to 328 K. The polarisation curves shown in Fig. 5 a and b illustrate that the corrosion current density (icorr) increases with temperature in both systems, indicating accelerated corrosion. In the absence of PNO2 (Fig. 5a), icorr increases from 3850 μA/cm2 at 298 K to 5715 μA/cm2 at 328 K, confirming that the corrosive effect of HCl intensifies at elevated temperatures. However, in the presence of PNO2 (Fig. 5b), the increase in icorr is less pronounced, with values ranging from 600 μA/cm2 at 298 K to 1194 μA/cm2 at 328 K, suggesting that PNO2 retains its inhibitory effect across the tested temperatures. Despite this, the inhibition efficiency (ηPP) decreases slightly with temperature, from 84.4 % at 298 K to 79.1 % at 328 K, which may be attributed to partial desorption of the inhibitor molecules from the mild steel surface [60,61].

The electrochemical parameters listed in Table 9 reveal a slight shift in the corrosion potential (-Ecorr) toward less negative values as temperature increases, both in the absence and presence of PNO2 , indicating that the fundamental reaction mechanism remains unchanged. Similarly, the Tafel slopes (−βc and βa) show minimal variations with temperature, confirming that PNO2 continues to act as a mixed-type inhibitor, affecting both anodic and cathodic processes. Although the inhibition efficiency of PNO2 decreases moderately at higher temperatures, the protective effect remains significant, highlighting the stability of the inhibitor under different thermal conditions [62,63].

The influence of temperature on the corrosion behaviour of mild steel in 15 % HCl with and without PNO2 was further assessed by evaluating the activation parameters using the Arrhenius and transition state equations. Fig. 6 a shows the Arrhenius plots of ln (icorr) versus 1000/T, where the slope of the linear fit was used to determine the activation energy (E
a ) values. The calculated E
a for the uninhibited solution is 10.7 kJ/mol, whereas in the presence of PNO2 , Ea increases to 18.6 kJ/mol, indicating that PNO2 raises the energy barrier required for the corrosion process. This increase confirms that the addition of PNO2 makes the corrosion reaction thermodynamically less favorable, which slows down the metal dissolution rate [64,65].

The transition state plots presented in Fig. 6b illustrate the variation of ln (icorr/T) as a function of 1000/T. The enthalpy of activation (ΔHa) and entropy of activation (ΔSa) values were derived from the linear regression of these plots. The results, summarised in Table 10 , indicate that ΔHa increases from 8.1 kJ/mol in 15 % HCl to 16.0 kJ/mol in the presence of PNO2 , suggesting that the inhibitor enhances the enthalpy barrier, reducing the corrosion rate. Moreover, the entropy of activation (ΔSa) becomes less negative in the inhibited solution, changing from −149.6 J/mol. K to −138.5 J/mol. K, indicating a more ordered transition state due to the adsorption of PNO2 molecules onto the mild steel surface. The increase in ΔHa and the less negative ΔSa values confirm that PNO2 slows down the corrosion reaction by modifying the systems thermodynamic parameters [42,66].

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analysis was performed to evaluate the surface morphology of mild steel after 6 h of immersion in 15 % HCl solution, both in the absence and presence of 10−3 M of PNO2 . As shown in Fig. 7 , the uninhibited sample (15 % HCl) displays a severely damaged surface characterized by pronounced topographical irregularities, with roughness values reaching Ra = 362 nm and Sa = 473 nm, indicating intense corrosion. In contrast, the specimen treated with PNO2 exhibits a notably smoother and more uniform surface, with significantly reduced roughness values (Ra = 119 nm, Sa = 159 nm). This reduction confirms the formation of a protective film that limits acid attack. Although the scans were performed over micrometer-scale areas (50 μm × 50 μm), the roughness measurements obtained reflect nanometer-scale surface variations, highlighting the inhibitor's effectiveness in preserving the structural integrity of mild steel [67–69].

The surface morphology of mild steel samples, both with and without inhibitor, was investigated using the SEM at 298 K following immersion in the corrosive media. Fig. 8 displays the linked photos combined. The surface image in the blank solution shows the harm done to the mild steel sample by the 15 % HCl solution's violent attack. It is clear that the surface has several flaws, including cracks and corrosion deposits.

The aggressive character of the chloride ions' interaction with the metal surface is responsible for the corrosion of the mild steel in the hydrochloric media [70,71]. However, the region morphology is drastically changed by the addition of the PNO2 inhibitor. A decrease in the rate of corrosion is shown by the steel region being smoother and less corroded. This improvement is due to the formation of a protective inhibitor layer on the metal surface, which shields the steel from more corrosive effects.

The surface composition of steel specimens in the uninhibited and inhibited media was examined using EDX spectroscopy. In Fig. 8, the EDX spectra are displayed. The composition of the elements present at the steel surface was indicated by the large signal for Fe and the tiny peak for C and O that we saw after the samples were submerged in a 15 % HCl solution. indicating that the surface oxygen abundance could be attributed to the formation of iron oxides [70,71]. But in the presence of PNO2 , the EDX spectrum signals linked to the corrosion products all but vanished. In the chemical composition of the organic molecule, we can see the appearance of a characteristic N peak. These results illustrate the inhibitor's ability to adsorb onto the metal and its significant inhibitory potential in an acidic environment.

The present work offers a detailed insight into the corrosion protection potential of a newly designed benzodiazepine derivative under highly acidic conditions. By combining electrochemical and surface analysis techniques, this study delivers a multi-angle evaluation of the compound's effectiveness and its interaction with the metal surface.
•
The compound exhibited excellent inhibition efficiency in 15 % HCl solution, particularly at 10−3M, reducing the corrosion rate considerably compared to the uninhibited system.
•
Electrochemical measurements revealed that the molecule interferes with both anodic and cathodic reactions, confirming a mixed-mode inhibition mechanism.
•
The impedance results reflected a significant increase in charge transfer resistance, suggesting the formation of a stable protective layer at the metal/electrolyte interface.
•
Adsorption studies confirmed that PNO2 obeys the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, with ΔG° ads values supporting a spontaneous chemisorption process.
•
AFM surface scans indicated a marked reduction in surface roughness in the presence of the inhibitor, consistent with a smoother and more compact surface layer.
•
The inhibitor's ability to shield mild steel in 15 % HCl was demonstrated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images, which showed a uniform surface of mild steel in the presence of PNO2.
The compound also retained notable activity at higher temperatures, supporting its potential use in demanding industrial environments.

In summary, the findings underline the relevance of benzodiazepine-based molecules as reliable corrosion inhibitors in harsh media, offering both efficiency and stability under aggressive chemical exposure.
